mnemonics.title: CONTIGUOUS ALLOCATION
mnemonics.content: C - CONTIGUOUS blocks, FAST sequential, FRAGMENTATION external, SIZE known first

mnemonics.title: LINKED ALLOCATION
mnemonics.content: L - LINKED list of blocks, NO external fragmentation, RANDOM access is SLOW, pointer in each block

mnemonics.title: INDEXED ALLOCATION
mnemonics.content: I - INDEX block with pointers, BOTH sequential and direct access, BLOCK pointers in one place

acronyms.usage: Used in linked allocation to track which blocks belong to each file by storing the next block pointer for each block in the file
Unix file system structure containing file metadata and pointers (direct, indirect) to data blocks
Operating system structure that maintains information about a file including its allocation information
Early OS that used FAT file system with linked allocation

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a row of consecutive lockers (disk blocks). Each file occupies a continuous stretch of adjacent lockers. Like a textbook taking up pages 10-15.
Imagine each locker contains not just your stuff but also a note saying 'Next item is in locker #47'. Files are like treasure hunt clues scattered across different lockers.
Imagine a table of contents (index block) at the front of a book. Each entry in the TOC points directly to the page (disk block) where that section is located. You can flip to any page instantly.
Imagine a stadium seating chart where green dots show available seats and red dots show taken seats. One bit per seat tells you immediately if you can sit there.

analogies.analogy: Parking a car in a shopping mall lot: You need to find 3 consecutive empty spots. If the lot is full of scattered empty spots but none have 3 in a row, you can't park even though there are enough total spaces.
A scavenger hunt where each clue leads to the next location. To find the 10th clue, you must visit all 9 previous locations first.
A book's index at the back. To find information about 'file allocation', you look at the index entry which immediately tells you the exact page numbers, without reading the whole book.
Trying to fit a 3-piece sofa through a door when all your empty rooms have small furniture scattered - you have enough total space but not in one usable piece.
